content.text: Based on the official Claude documentation,
"Subagents & Hooks"
in the Agent SDK refers to two powerful patterns for building complex AI workflows:
Subagents
delegate tasks to specialized assistant agents, while
Hooks
intercept and control agent behavior at specific points in the execution lifecycle.
Subagents
What They Are
Subagents are assistant agents that can be used to delegate tasks
within your agent workflow.

Main Agent (orchestrator)
    ↓
Delegates to Subagent A (research specialist)
Delegates to Subagent B (coding specialist)
Delegates to Subagent C (review specialist)
    ↓
Subagents complete tasks
    ↓
Results aggregated by main agent
Key capability
: "Subagent orchestration lets you delegate tasks to specialized assistant agents".
When to Use Subagents
Scenario
Why Subagents Work
Multi-agent workflows
Different agents for different roles (software engineer, architect, SRE, tester, UX designer, product manager)
Parallel execution
Subagents can work simultaneously on different aspects of a task
Specialized expertise
Each subagent has unique skills/prompts for specific domains
Complex tasks
Break down large tasks into smaller, manageable pieces
How to Create Subagents
Subagent Communication
Main agent sends task → Subagent processes → Returns result → Main agent integrates
Pattern
: "Loop through these roles in your script, giving each agent a turn to work on a shared project goal".
Hooks
What They Are
Hooks intercept and control agent behavior
at specific points in the execution lifecycle.

Agent lifecycle:
User Prompt → PreToolUse → Tool Execution → PostToolUse → Result
     ↓          ↓             ↓              ↓           ↓
Hook       Hook          Hook           Hook        Hook
Key purpose
: "Hooks allow you to intercept and modify agent behavior without retraining the model".
Hook Events
Hook Type
When It Fires
UserPromptSubmit
Before agent processes user prompt
PreToolUse
Before agent executes a tool call
PostToolUse
After agent completes a tool call
PreCompact
Before agent compacts context
Stop
When agent stops/shuts down
SubagentStop
When a subagent stops
[docs](https://docs.rs/claude-agent-sdk-rs/latest/claude_agent_sdk_rs/types/hooks/index.html)
How to Use Hooks
Hook Use Cases
Use Case
Implementation
Enforce rules
"Read before write" without retraining model
Debugging
Log all tool calls and results
Security
Validate tool arguments before execution
Determinism
Intercept hallucinated responses, inject feedback
Example

: "If an agent hallucinates or skips a step (e.g., guessing a Pokemon stat instead of checking a script), a hook can intercept the response and inject feedback: 'Please make sure you write a script, please make sure you read this data.'".
Combining Subagents & Hooks
Pattern: Subagents with Hook Guards
Mental Model
Think of
subagents
like
managing a team

: you're the project manager (main agent), and you delegate tasks to specialists (subagents) — one for research, one for coding, one for testing. Each specialist works independently on their portion, then you combine the results.
Think of
hooks
like
security checkpoints

in an airport: before the agent passes through each checkpoint (tool call, prompt submission), the hook checks if it's safe, modifies it if needed, or blocks it entirely. The agent still goes through the process, but with guardrails.
Key Takeaways
Subagents = assistant agents for delegating tasks within agent workflow
Subagent orchestration = delegate to specialized assistant agents
Use subagents for: multi-agent workflows, parallel execution, specialized expertise, complex tasks
Create subagents:
new Subagent({ instructions })
mainAgent.delegate(task, subagent)
Hooks = intercept and control agent behavior at specific lifecycle points
Hook events: UserPromptSubmit, PreToolUse, PostToolUse, PreCompact, Stop, SubagentStop
Create hooks:
new Hooks({ preToolUse, postToolUse, userPromptSubmit })
+ pass to
Agent({ hooks })
Hook use cases: enforce rules, debugging, security, determinism
Example: Hook intercepts hallucination, injects feedback ("write script, read data") without retraining
Combine: Subagents with hook guards for security/validation
"Subagents and Hooks expose infrastructure behind Claude Code as a library" — enabling complex multi-agent orchestration and behavior control patterns.
Best Practices
When working with subagents & hooks, following established best practices ensures reliability, maintainability, and optimal performance.
Start simple and iterate — begin with the minimum viable implementation before adding complexity.
Test with representative inputs — ensure your test cases cover edge cases, not just the happy path.
Monitor performance — track latency, token usage, and error rates in production environments.
Version your prompts and configurations — treat prompt text like source code with proper version control.
Document your design decisions — record why you chose specific approaches for future maintainers.
Plan for failure — implement graceful degradation when the AI component is unavailable or returns unexpected results.
Review outputs regularly — periodically audit what the system produces to catch quality drift over time.
Common Mistakes to Avoid
These pitfalls frequently trip up developers working with subagents & hooks. Knowing them in advance saves significant debugging time.
Assuming deterministic output — language models are probabilistic; the same input can produce different outputs across calls. Always validate the structure of responses.
Ignoring token limits — exceeding context window limits causes truncation or errors. Track token counts, especially in multi-turn conversations.
Over-engineering early — building complex infrastructure before validating basic functionality wastes effort. Get a working prototype first.
Hardcoding prompts in code — embed prompts in configuration files or databases so non-developers can iterate on them.
Skipping error handling — API calls fail due to rate limits, timeouts, or content policy violations. Always wrap calls in try/catch with appropriate retry logic.
Performance and Scalability
As your usage of subagents & hooks scales, these performance factors become increasingly important.
Latency budget — typical API latency ranges from 1-30 seconds depending on model size and output length. Design your UX around streaming to provide early feedback.
Concurrency limits — each API tier has request-per-minute (RPM) and token-per-minute (TPM) limits. Implement request queuing for burst traffic.
Prompt caching — use the cache_control breakpoint on stable, repeated prompt prefixes to reduce costs by up to 90% and latency by 85%.
Batching — for non-interactive workloads, the Message Batches API processes up to 10,000 requests asynchronously at 50% cost reduction.
Model selection — use Claude Haiku for high-volume, low-complexity tasks and Claude Sonnet or Opus for complex reasoning where quality matters more than speed.
Best Practices
When working with subagents & hooks, following established best practices ensures reliability, maintainability, and optimal performance.
Start simple and iterate — begin with the minimum viable implementation before adding complexity.
Test with representative inputs — ensure your test cases cover edge cases, not just the happy path.
Monitor performance — track latency, token usage, and error rates in production environments.
Version your prompts and configurations — treat prompt text like source code with proper version control.
Document your design decisions — record why you chose specific approaches for future maintainers.
Plan for failure — implement graceful degradation when the AI component is unavailable or returns unexpected results.
Review outputs regularly — periodically audit what the system produces to catch quality drift over time.
Common Mistakes to Avoid
These pitfalls frequently trip up developers working with subagents & hooks. Knowing them in advance saves significant debugging time.
Assuming deterministic output — language models are probabilistic; the same input can produce different outputs across calls. Always validate the structure of responses.
Ignoring token limits — exceeding context window limits causes truncation or errors. Track token counts, especially in multi-turn conversations.
Over-engineering early — building complex infrastructure before validating basic functionality wastes effort. Get a working prototype first.
Hardcoding prompts in code — embed prompts in configuration files or databases so non-developers can iterate on them.
Skipping error handling — API calls fail due to rate limits, timeouts, or content policy violations. Always wrap calls in try/catch with appropriate retry logic.
Performance and Scalability
As your usage of subagents & hooks scales, these performance factors become increasingly important.
Latency budget — typical API latency ranges from 1-30 seconds depending on model size and output length. Design your UX around streaming to provide early feedback.
Concurrency limits — each API tier has request-per-minute (RPM) and token-per-minute (TPM) limits. Implement request queuing for burst traffic.
Prompt caching — use the cache_control breakpoint on stable, repeated prompt prefixes to reduce costs by up to 90% and latency by 85%.
Batching — for non-interactive workloads, the Message Batches API processes up to 10,000 requests asynchronously at 50% cost reduction.
Model selection — use Claude Haiku for high-volume, low-complexity tasks and Claude Sonnet or Opus for complex reasoning where quality matters more than speed.
Designing Effective Subagent Boundaries

The hardest part of building a multi-agent system is deciding where to draw the boundaries between subagents. Boundaries that are too coarse produce subagents that are too complex to reason about and debug. Boundaries that are too fine produce communication overhead that dominates runtime. A good boundary has three properties: a single, well-defined responsibility; clear input/output contracts; and minimal shared state with other subagents.

A useful design exercise is to write the subagent's system prompt before writing any code. If you cannot write a concise system prompt that describes exactly what the subagent does, the boundary is wrong. A system prompt that requires three paragraphs of caveats usually indicates a subagent that is doing two different jobs.

Shared state is the most common source of subagent boundary problems. If two subagents both need to read and write the same data structure, consider merging them into one or introducing an explicit "state manager" subagent that owns the data and exposes it via tool calls. Passing state through the orchestrator's messages is usually cleaner than shared external storage, especially during development.

import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

# Subagent 1: Research - only retrieves information, never writes
RESEARCH_SYSTEM = """You are a research assistant.
Your only job is to find and summarize relevant information.
You have access to web search and knowledge base tools.
You do NOT write code, generate creative content, or make decisions.
Always cite your sources in your response."""

# Subagent 2: Synthesizer - combines research into structured output
SYNTH_SYSTEM = """You are a synthesis specialist.
You receive research summaries and produce structured analysis documents.
You do NOT do research yourself - work only with provided research.
Output must be in the exact format requested by the orchestrator."""

def run_research_subagent(query: str, tools: list) -> str:
    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-opus-4-5",
        max_tokens=2048,
        system=RESEARCH_SYSTEM,
        tools=tools,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": f"Research this topic thoroughly: {query}"}]
    )
    # In practice, handle tool_use blocks in a loop here
    return response.content[-1].text if response.content else ""

def run_synthesis_subagent(research: str, output_format: str) -> str:
    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-opus-4-5",
        max_tokens=2048,
        system=SYNTH_SYSTEM,
        messages=[{
            "role": "user",
            "content": f"Synthesize this research into {output_format}:\n\n{research}"
        }]
    )
    return response.content[0].text

def orchestrate(topic: str, tools: list) -> dict:
    """Orchestrator that coordinates research and synthesis subagents."""
    research = run_research_subagent(topic, tools)
    analysis = run_synthesis_subagent(research, "a structured JSON report")
    return {"topic": topic, "research": research, "analysis": analysis}
Passing Context Between Subagents

Context passing is how subagents share information. There are three patterns: direct message passing (orchestrator passes previous subagent output as user message), tool-based retrieval (each subagent calls a shared tool to read/write structured state), and structured handoff objects (orchestrator produces a typed schema that the receiving subagent unpacks).

For most applications, direct message passing is the right starting point. The orchestrator calls subagent A, extracts the result, and includes it in the user message to subagent B. This is easy to debug because the entire communication is visible in the message history. The main downside is token cost: large subagent outputs passed between many subagents can be expensive. In those cases, structured handoff objects that contain only the necessary fields are more efficient.

Never assume that a subagent can "remember" what a previous subagent did. Claude does not share memory across separate API calls. Each subagent call starts fresh. All context the subagent needs must be present in its system prompt or in the user message you send it.
Pass only the information the receiving subagent actually needs, not the full output of the previous subagent
Use structured formats (JSON or XML) for handoffs between subagents to make parsing reliable
Include a brief summary of the overall task goal in every subagent system prompt for coherence
When chaining more than 3 subagents, consider passing a running "task summary" object that each subagent updates
Log every inter-subagent message during development—this is your primary debugging tool
Define explicit "no result" and "error" states in your handoff schemas so downstream agents handle them gracefully
Hook-Based Lifecycle Management

Hooks are lifecycle callbacks that the Agent SDK fires at specific points in an agent's execution: before and after each model call, before and after each tool call, on error, and on completion. They are the correct mechanism for cross-cutting concerns: logging, rate limiting, safety checks, and metric collection.

A key design principle: hooks should be observational by default and interventional only when necessary. An observational hook records what happened and returns. An interventional hook can modify or block behavior, which introduces complexity. Prefer logging hooks during development, then add intervention only for validated safety requirements.

import anthropic
import logging
import time

logger = logging.getLogger("agent")

class AgentHooks:
    def __init__(self, agent_name: str):
        self.agent_name = agent_name
        self._call_start_time = None

    def on_model_request(self, messages: list, system: str, **kwargs) -> None:
        """Called before each API request to Claude."""
        self._call_start_time = time.time()
        token_estimate = sum(len(m.get("content", "")) for m in messages) // 4
        logger.info(f"[{self.agent_name}] API call | est_tokens={token_estimate}")

    def on_model_response(self, response: anthropic.types.Message) -> None:
        """Called after each API response from Claude."""
        elapsed = time.time() - (self._call_start_time or time.time())
        usage = response.usage
        logger.info(
            f"[{self.agent_name}] Response | "
            f"input={usage.input_tokens} output={usage.output_tokens} "
            f"latency={elapsed:.2f}s"
        )

    def on_tool_call(self, tool_name: str, tool_input: dict) -> dict | None:
        """Called before each tool execution. Return None to allow, return dict to override."""
        logger.info(f"[{self.agent_name}] Tool call: {tool_name} | input_keys={list(tool_input.keys())}")

        # Safety check: block dangerous tool combinations
        BLOCKED_TOOLS = {"delete_database", "send_bulk_email"}
        if tool_name in BLOCKED_TOOLS:
            logger.warning(f"[{self.agent_name}] Blocked tool: {tool_name}")
            return {"error": f"Tool {tool_name} is not permitted for this agent"}

        return None  # allow the call

    def on_tool_result(self, tool_name: str, result: dict) -> None:
        """Called after each tool execution completes."""
        status = "ok" if "error" not in result else "error"
        logger.info(f"[{self.agent_name}] Tool result: {tool_name} | status={status}")

    def on_error(self, error: Exception) -> None:
        """Called when the agent encounters an unhandled error."""
        logger.error(f"[{self.agent_name}] Error: {type(error).__name__}: {error}")

# Usage
hooks = AgentHooks("research-agent")
Subagent Isolation and Security

In production systems, different subagents often need different permission levels. A research subagent that only reads from a knowledge base should not have write permissions. A code-execution subagent needs sandboxing. An email-sending subagent should require human confirmation before sending. Designing permission levels per subagent—rather than giving all subagents the same tool set—is a core security practice.
Define a minimal tool set for each subagent based on the principle of least privilege
Use separate API keys per subagent if tools require authentication, to enable per-subagent audit logging
Run code-execution subagents in sandboxed environments (Docker, gVisor, or cloud functions) with network and filesystem restrictions
For high-stakes actions (email, payments, database writes), require a "confirmation" step before the subagent proceeds
Log every subagent's tool calls to a centralized audit log keyed by request ID for end-to-end traceability
Test subagents in isolation before integrating them into the orchestration pipeline
